reference: GATE CS — Compiler Design / Backend Phases

map.condition: Question about ILC / symbol table pass in assembler
map.use: \text{Pass 1: ILC tracks } \text{address}; \text{ Pass 2: generates object code}

map.condition: Converting expression to three-address code or quadruple
Minimising temporaries / common sub-expressions via DAG
Jump targets unknown at first pass — use backpatching
Resolving external symbols across object modules

formulas.name: Pass 1 Activities of a Two-Pass Assembler
formulas.latex: \text{Pass 1: } \begin{cases} \text{Build symbol table (labels} \to \text{addresses)} \\ \text{Add literals to literal table} \\ \text{Advance ILC for each instruction/directive} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Pass 1: build symbol table, add literals, advance ILC (Instruction Location Counter).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which assembler activities happen in Pass 1 vs Pass 2.

terms.means: Instruction Location Counter — tracks current address being assembled
Maps label names to their memory addresses

formulas.trap: Listing is printed in Pass 2 (not Pass 1) because object code is not yet available in Pass 1.

formulas.name: Pass 2 Activities of a Two-Pass Assembler
formulas.plain: Pass 2: generate object code, print listing, assign and output literal values.
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying which activities are deferred to Pass 2.

terms.means: Machine/binary instructions output by the assembler

formulas.trap: Address resolution of local symbols happens in Pass 1 (via ILC); object code is generated in Pass 2.

formulas.name: Instruction Location Counter (ILC)
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever asked about the purpose or update rule of the ILC in an assembler.

terms.means: Instruction Location Counter — address of the next instruction to be assigned
Number of bytes occupied by instruction/directive I

formulas.trap: ILC is NOT the program counter at runtime; it is a compile-time counter used during assembly.

formulas.name: Macro Expansion and Infinite Loop Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Infinite loop iff macro } M \text{ expands to a call of } M \text{ (directly or indirectly) with a non-terminating argument sequence}
formulas.plain: A macro causes an infinite loop if its expansion calls itself (or another macro that calls it back) without a base case that terminates.
formulas.whenToUse: When analyzing whether a macro definition leads to non-terminating expansion.

terms.means: Macro names in a mutual or self-recursive expansion
Conditional assembly directives that may or may not halt recursion

formulas.trap: The loop occurs at assembly time (macro expansion), not at runtime.

formulas.name: Three-Address Code — Quadruple Form
formulas.plain: (op, arg1, arg2, result)  — four fields per instruction
formulas.whenToUse: When writing intermediate code for arithmetic/boolean expressions as quadruples.

terms.means: Operator (e.g., +, -, *, goto, if)
Source operands (may be empty for unary ops or goto)
Destination temporary or label

formulas.trap: In a quadruple, the result field holds the destination; in a triple, the instruction index is the implicit result — don't confuse them.

formulas.name: Three-Address Code — Triple Form
formulas.latex: (i):\; (\,op,\; arg_1,\; arg_2\,) \quad \text{result is implicitly statement } i
formulas.plain: (i): (op, arg1, arg2)  — result referenced by instruction index i
formulas.whenToUse: When the question uses triple notation and asks about referencing intermediate results.

terms.means: Statement/instruction number used as implicit result reference

formulas.trap: Triples cannot be easily reordered (optimisation) because references are by position; indirect triples solve this.

formulas.name: DAG Construction for Basic Blocks
formulas.plain: DAG nodes = unique sub-expressions + unique leaf nodes; shared common sub-expressions collapse to one node.
formulas.whenToUse: When counting DAG nodes for a set of quadruples / expressions.

terms.means: Directed Acyclic Graph — nodes shared for common sub-expressions
Node representing a variable or constant (no children)
Node representing an operation; shared if CSE exists

formulas.trap: If A+B appears twice, there is ONE interior node for it (shared), not two. Count unique sub-expressions carefully.

formulas.name: Code Template for if-then-else
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{l} \text{code for } E \\ \texttt{if } E \texttt{ goto } L_1 \\ \text{code for } S_2 \quad (\text{else branch}) \\ \texttt{goto } L_2 \\ L_1\text{: code for } S_1 \quad (\text{then branch}) \\ L_2\text{: } \ldots \end{array}
formulas.plain: Evaluate E; if true goto L1 (then-branch); fall through to else-branch; goto L2; L1: then-branch code; L2: ...
formulas.whenToUse: When generating or verifying TAC/quadruples for if-then-else constructs.

terms.means: Label for start of then-branch
Label for end of if-then-else (continuation)

formulas.trap: A common error is jumping to the then-branch and falling through to the else-branch, reversing the logic — check which branch is the fall-through.

formulas.name: Boolean Expression TAC (No Short-Circuit)
formulas.latex: \text{Each operand evaluated fully; result stored; then conditional jump:} \quad \texttt{if } t \texttt{ goto } L_T \texttt{ else goto } L_F
formulas.plain: Without short-circuit: evaluate all sub-expressions fully, store in temporaries, then perform conditional jump.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to generate TAC for a boolean expression without partial (short-circuit) evaluation.

terms.means: Temporary holding the boolean result
True and False jump targets

formulas.trap: If the problem says 'partial evaluation not permitted', you must NOT use short-circuit jumps; evaluate every sub-expression.

formulas.name: Backpatching — Core Idea
formulas.latex: \text{truelist}(E) = \{i \mid \text{instruction } i \text{ is an unresolved jump for true outcome of } E\}
formulas.plain: truelist(E) = list of quadruple indices with unfilled jump targets for the true outcome; backpatch fills them once target is known.
formulas.whenToUse: When questions ask about one-pass intermediate code generation for boolean/control-flow expressions.

terms.means: List of quadruples with goto __ that should jump when expression is true
List of quadruples with goto __ that should jump when expression is false
Fill all unresolved jumps in list with target label

formulas.trap: Backpatching allows one-pass code generation for boolean expressions AND control-flow statements — both statements I and II in GATE questions are typically TRUE.

formulas.name: Merging Backlists (Boolean Connectives)
formulas.plain: For E1 OR E2: truelist = truelist(E1) union truelist(E2); falselist = falselist(E2). For E1 AND E2: falselist = falselist(E1) union falselist(E2); truelist = truelist(E2).
formulas.whenToUse: When combining backpatch lists for compound boolean expressions with AND/OR.

terms.means: Concatenation / union of jump-instruction index lists

formulas.trap: For OR: falselist(E1) is backpatched to the start of E2 (not to the final false target) — the merge only applies to the outer result lists.

formulas.name: Key Tasks of a Linker (Link Editor)
formulas.latex: \text{Linker: } \begin{cases} \text{1. Symbol resolution — match external refs to definitions} \\ \text{2. Relocation — adjust addresses of object modules} \\ \text{3. Combine object modules} \to \text{executable} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Linker: (1) resolve external symbol references across modules, (2) relocate addresses, (3) combine object modules into an executable.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what a linker/link editor does or what happens at link time.

terms.means: Symbol used in one module but defined in another
Adjusting address values in code/data when modules are placed at runtime addresses

formulas.trap: Macro parameter matching is done by the macro assembler (not the linker). A linker does NOT interpret macro calls.

formulas.name: Reference Resolution Time for Cross-Module Calls
formulas.plain: If module M1 references a function defined in M2, the reference is resolved at link time (not compile time, not run time).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked at what stage an inter-module function reference is resolved.

terms.means: The stage at which the linker combines object files and resolves cross-module symbols

formulas.trap: Do not confuse with dynamic linking: for shared/dynamic libraries the resolution can be deferred to load time or even run time.

formulas.name: Static vs Dynamic (Shared) Linking
formulas.latex: \text{Dynamic linking advantages: } \begin{cases} \text{Smaller executable size} \\ \text{Library updates without relinking} \\ \text{Shared pages} \Rightarrow \text{less memory} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Dynamic linking: smaller executables, shared library pages, easy library updates. Disadvantage: does NOT guarantee lesser page faults (may increase them if shared library is not in memory).
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing static and dynamic/shared linking in terms of executable size, memory, and page faults.

terms.means: Library code is copied into the executable at link time
Library code is loaded at run time; multiple processes share one copy

formulas.trap: Lesser overall page fault rate is NOT guaranteed by dynamic linking — this is a common GATE trap answer listed as an 'advantage'.

formulas.name: Loader — Relocation
formulas.latex: \text{Effective address} = \text{base address (load address)} + \text{relative offset in object module}
formulas.plain: Effective address = base/load address + relative offset. The loader patches all relocatable addresses using the relocation table.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about how a loader adjusts addresses when placing a program in memory.

terms.means: Starting address where the program/module is loaded in memory
Offset within the module as recorded in the object file
Table in object file identifying all addresses that need adjustment

formulas.trap: Relocation is needed even for programs compiled with absolute addressing if they are loaded at a different address than assumed.
